summary: Julia Evans reflects on her first three months working remotely for Stripe from the east coast while her team is in San Francisco. She honestly catalogs the difficulties, including timezone challenges, missing synchronous communication, and not knowing coworkers beyond IRC names. She also identifies genuine upsides like flexibility, working in the sun, and learning to optimize for happiness. The post frames remote work as a tradeoff worth making rather than an ideal arrangement, with practical strategies for coping.
key_points: Timezone differences create both problems (no one to talk to early morning) and opportunities (uninterrupted focus time, handoff advantage)
Losing synchronous communication was harder than expected, especially for building technical consensus
Having other remote teammates is critical — being the only remote person would likely be unsustainable
Happiness while remote is measurable and optimizable, correlating directly with time spent talking to people
Reframing remote work as 'a cool possibility with ups and downs' rather than a loss makes a meaningful difference
money_quotes: I didn't realize how much I depended on synchronous communication (talking face-to-face!) to do things until it was taken away from me.
My happiness seems to be proportional to the amount of time I spend talking to people. This is something I can measure and optimize!
Thinking about working remote as 'a cool possibility with some ups and downs' instead of 'this enemy that means I HAVE TO SEE LESS PEOPLE OH NO' helps me be happy instead of grumpy.
A lot of people are just names on IRC to me. I do not know of any upside to this, or how to fix it.
If I were the only one it would probably be a disaster and I would be way more sad.
I changed my work computer's clock to be the time in San Francisco. This helps more than I expected.
key_insight: Remote work is sustainable when you treat happiness as a measurable variable, have other remote teammates, and reframe the tradeoffs as choices rather than losses.
